claim: Hand A's Layer-2 outer morphemes {y, n, r, ol, l} and word-initial markers (qo, da-) match documented medieval Latin scribal abbreviation conventions (Cappelli's Lexicon Abbreviaturarum) in their positional and combinatorial behavior. The test is STRUCTURAL, not a decoding: if Brain-V's observed positional, selectional, and frequency properties align with documented Latin abbreviation conventions at an abstract level, the scribal-abbreviation hypothesis (candidate (a) from the 15th-c. analysis) gains material support.
evidence_for: H-BV-SUFFIX-SEQUENCE-01 identified Layer-2 outers {y, n, r, ol, l} with closed-inventory 86% coverage — analogous in scale to the closed inventory of Latin scribal abbreviation marks (Tironian -us, macron, -rum, -bus, -orum/-arum) used in 15th-c. manuscripts

H-BV-SUFFIX-SUBCLASS-01 established strict selectional restrictions between Layer-1 sub-slots and Layer-2 outers — matching the structural behavior of Latin abbreviation conventions where certain marks only attach to certain stem/vowel contexts (e.g., macron only over vowels)

H-BV-HAND-B-SUBCLASS-01 replicated the paradigm across both scribal hands, consistent with the field-wide observation that scribal abbreviation conventions persisted across scribes with lexical-frequency variation
Brain-V's earlier finding that qo- is 98.9% word-initial matches Latin's qu- word-initial distribution (quod, quae, qui, quam, quo, etc. are among the most common Latin word-initial clusters)
EVA 'y' has a well-known visual resemblance to the Tironian -us 9-shape, the single most common terminal abbreviation in 15th-c. Latin scribal practice
evidence_against: Cappelli's Lexicon Abbreviaturarum catalogues hundreds of scribal abbreviation forms; any Voynich outer can be mapped to some Latin abbreviation with sufficient imagination. Must be careful that matches are DIAGNOSTIC (unique) rather than GENERIC (any outer could fit any mark)

Latin scribal abbreviation marks are defined ON TOP OF a Latin plaintext. If Voynich is Latin plus abbreviations, the underlying Latin must independently explain the stem system, the productivity pattern, and the hapax ratio — none of which have been tested against Latin yet
The test cannot distinguish 'Latin scribal abbreviation' from 'constructed system inspired by Latin scribal abbreviation'. A positive result is consistent with both
test:
LOCKED PROCEDURE.

  DATA: Hand A tokens under Tokenisation A (MULTI_GLYPHS + 'ol'), full corpus (not restricted to N>=3). Also word-initial qo- markers from full Hand A corpus.

  STEP 1 — OBSERVED PROFILE per Layer-2 outer o in {y, n, r, ol, l}:
    POS-A: fraction of o-occurrences that are word-terminal (last glyph-unit of the word).
    POS-B: fraction of o-occurrences that are word-medial.
    SEL-A: from SUFFIX-SUBCLASS-01 — diff(V/C) ratio, i.e., which inner sub-class o prefers.
    SEL-B: absolute count of o-attachments on vowel-class vs consonant-class inners.
    FREQ: overall frequency of o across Hand A Layer-2 positions (i.e., count(o as outer) / total outer positions).

  STEP 2 — LATIN REFERENCE CONVENTIONS (pre-specified from Cappelli 1899, standard 15th-c. manuscript scholarship):
    L1 — Tironian -us (9-shape):
      POSITION: word-terminal only (replaces declension ending -us, -um, rarely -us in adverbs).
      SELECTION: follows noun/adjective stems of any type (consonant- or vowel-final in underlying Latin).
      FREQUENCY: very high in Latin scribal texts (estimate 10-20% of word-terminal positions in herbal/pharmaceutical Latin based on standard concordances).

    L2 — Macron / overline (nasal suspension):
      POSITION: over a vowel, indicating omitted nasal (n or m). The mark can appear at any position in the word that contains a vowel.
      SELECTION: strictly over vowels; structurally impossible over consonants (you cannot have a nasal suspension where there is no vowel).
      FREQUENCY: very high (nasals are frequently omitted; ~10-15% of tokens in typical scribal Latin).

    L3 — -rum / -orum / -arum suspensions:
      POSITION: word-terminal only (genitive plural endings).
      SELECTION: always follows a stem vowel — the ending is -Vrum where V is o, a, or (rarely) e/u.
      FREQUENCY: moderate-to-high (genitive plural is common in catalogue-style texts like herbals).

    L4 — -bus (dative/ablative plural):
      POSITION: word-terminal only.
      SELECTION: follows stems ending in consonant or i-theme (3rd decl consonant stems: patri-bus, regi-bus; 4th decl: manu-bus/manibus). Primarily consonant-preferring.
      FREQUENCY: moderate (specific to dative/ablative plural contexts).

    L5 — -lis / -alis / -ilis adjectival:
      POSITION: word-terminal.
      SELECTION: follows stem vowel for adjectival derivation (-alis: stem ending with a; -ilis: ending with i; -olis: ending with o).
      FREQUENCY: moderate (common in descriptive/adjectival registers like herbals).

  STEP 3 — SCORE each Layer-2 outer against each Latin reference on three dimensions:
    POSITIONAL MATCH: 1 if Hand A POS-A >= 0.80 AND Latin reference is word-terminal, OR if Hand A POS-A between 0.40-0.80 AND Latin reference allows medial position. 0 otherwise.
    SELECTIONAL MATCH: 1 if Hand A SEL-A aligns directionally with Latin SELECTION predictions (e.g., Hand A V-preferring matches Latin 'follows vowels'; Hand A C-preferring matches Latin 'follows consonants'; Hand A V-exclusive matches Latin 'strictly over vowels'). 0 otherwise.
    FREQUENCY MATCH: 1 if Hand A FREQ is within plausible bounds of Latin reference FREQUENCY (>=5% for 'very high' references; 2-15% for 'moderate'; <=2% for 'rare'). 0 otherwise.
    A Hand A outer 'matches' a Latin reference if it scores >= 2 of 3 on that reference.
    A Hand A outer shows 'strong correspondence' if it matches at least one Latin reference at >=2/3.

  STEP 4 — WORD-INITIAL MARKERS qo- and da-:
    POSITIONAL: Hand A qo- is known to be 98.9% word-initial; compare against Latin qu- word-initial frequency (est. 5-10% of word-initial positions in Latin herbal/pharmaceutical texts).
    Report only; do NOT count toward the 3-of-5 Layer-2 decision (the decision rule is specifically about Layer-2 outers).

  DECISION RULE (LOCKED):
    CONFIRMED: 3 or more of 5 Layer-2 outers {y, n, r, ol, l} show strong correspondence (>=2/3 dimensions) to at least one Latin reference convention. Scribal-abbreviation hypothesis gains material support.
    MARGINAL: 1-2 outers match. Partial correspondence worth follow-up.
    REFUTED: 0 outers match. Scribal-abbreviation hypothesis weakens significantly.
test_metric: count of Layer-2 outers with strong correspondence to at least one Latin convention
test_threshold: CONFIRMED if >=3/5 outers match. MARGINAL if 1-2/5. REFUTED if 0/5.

tests_run.details:
CONFIRMED 4/5 under locked rule. 11022 Hand-A words analysed.

  PER-OUTER PROFILES (Hand A):
    outer  total  term.frac  L2-freq  V:C(count)    diff(V/C)
    y      4484   0.773      0.3145   1028:1508     0.256
    n      1801   0.946      0.1546   1697:0        inf (V-exclusive)
    r      2325   0.799      0.1686   1620:8        76.06
    ol     2149   0.735      0.1433   362:466       0.292
    l      860    0.549      0.0428   382:16        8.97

  PER-OUTER STRONG CORRESPONDENCE (>=2/3 dimensions on at least one Latin reference):

  y: NO MATCH. Best Latin ref: L1 Tironian -us at 1/3.
    Position 0.773 is just below the 0.80 terminal-only threshold (near miss).
    Frequency 0.3145 is ABOVE the 0.05-0.25 'very high' range — y is too frequent for any single Latin convention (no Latin abbreviation accounts for 31% of word-terminal positions by itself).
    Selection C-preferring (diff 0.256) matches only L4 -bus, which has low expected frequency (1-10%) that y violates.
    The y anomaly is notable: it is the most common Hand-A Layer-2 outer, and it does not fit any single Latin convention. A composite Latin explanation (e.g., y encodes multiple Latin terminations collapsed) would be needed.

  n: STRONG MATCH at 3/3 against L1 Tironian -us AND L2 macron-nasal AND L5 -lis (and 2/3 against L3 -rum).
    Position 0.946 terminal, well above threshold.
    Selection V-exclusive (0/1697 on C-inners corpus-wide for Hand A; 0/9784 across all samples) matches L2 macron-nasal's structural requirement (nasal suspension is impossible over a consonant) EXACTLY.
    Frequency 0.1546 in-range for L1 and L2.
    The n-exclusivity constraint's match to L2 macron-nasal is the single most structurally-specific finding in this test — nasal suspensions CANNOT occur over consonants in Latin orthography by definition.

  r: STRONG MATCH at 3/3 against L2 macron-nasal (and 2/3 against L1).
    Position 0.799 terminal (just below 0.80 for terminal-only rules; passes L2 flexible rule).
    Selection V-preferring at 76x — matches L2's vowel-only requirement and L3's stem-vowel requirement.
    Frequency 0.1686 in-range for L1, L2.

  ol: STRONG MATCH at 2/3 against L1 Tironian -us AND L2 macron-nasal.
    Position 0.735 terminal (below terminal-only 0.80; passes flexible 0.40).
    Selection C-preferring at 0.292 — matches L1 (any) and fails L2 (which requires vowel).
    Frequency 0.1433 in-range for L1, L2.
    ol's C-preferring behaviour and moderate frequency is consistent with a scribal ligature for a consonant-cluster termination such as -bus (though it fails L4's frequency range).

  l: STRONG MATCH at 2/3 against L2 macron-nasal AND L3 -rum AND L5 -lis.
    Position 0.549 terminal (below both rules' thresholds — l is notably medial-preferring, 0.451 medial, which distinguishes it from the other outers).
    Selection V-preferring at 8.97x — matches L2, L3, L5 vowel-selection rules.
    Frequency 0.0428 in-range for L3, L5.
    l's medial-preferring behaviour (relative to other outers) combined with V-preferring selection matches the -lis adjectival ending, which appears both word-medially (as part of compound forms) and word-finally.

  WORD-INITIAL MARKERS (descriptive, non-decision-feeding):
    qo- word-initial: 1066 / 11022 = 9.67% of Hand A words. Latin qu- word-initial estimated range: 5-10%. DIRECT MATCH.
    q-initial (any): 1103 / 11022 = 10.0%; qo-initial is 96.65% of all q-initial (consistent with prior 98.9% claim; small discrepancy likely due to tokenisation edge cases).
    da- word-initial: 1014 / 11022 = 9.20% of Hand A words. Latin d-initial (dare, de-, di-, dominus, dum-, dicere, etc.) is also very common as a word-initial family. Directional consistency.

  INTERPRETATION:
    4 of 5 Layer-2 outers (n, r, ol, l) show strong correspondence to documented Latin scribal abbreviation conventions. The single non-matching outer (y) has anomalously high frequency that exceeds any single Latin convention's expected range. qo- word-initial matches Latin qu- directly.
    Under the locked decision rule, >=3/5 outers matching is CONFIRMED. Result: 4/5 → CONFIRMED.
    Structural-level correspondence with Latin scribal practice is supported. This does NOT establish that Voynich IS Latin — a positive result is consistent with (a) Latin under scribal abbreviation, (b) constructed script inspired by Latin scribal practice, or (c) a different language written using Latin-style abbreviation conventions.
    The most structurally-specific finding is n ↔ macron-nasal: the vowel-exclusivity of 'n' mirrors the Latin orthographic impossibility of placing a nasal suspension over a consonant. This is a hard structural constraint, not a frequency coincidence.

verdict_note: CONFIRMED 4/5. Hand A Layer-2 outers n, r, ol, l show strong (>=2/3 dimension) correspondence to Latin scribal abbreviation conventions (Tironian -us, macron-nasal, -rum, -bus, -lis). Outer y does not match any single Latin convention due to its anomalously high 31% frequency. qo- word-initial matches Latin qu- (9.67% vs expected 5-10%). The sharpest finding: Hand A's 'n' vowel-exclusivity mirrors Latin macron-nasal's structural impossibility over consonants — a hard orthographic constraint, not a frequency coincidence. Result supports candidate (a) scribal-abbreviation from the 15th-c. synthesis; does NOT establish Voynich IS Latin — compatible with Latin under abbreviation, constructed script using scribal conventions, or a different language written with Latin-style marks.
findings_for_followup: The y anomaly is the single most informative failure: y's 31% terminal frequency exceeds any single Latin convention's range. Hypothesis for follow-up: y encodes a COMPOSITE of multiple Latin terminations (e.g., -us + -um + -is collapsed into one glyph at the scribal level). Test: partition y-final words by preceding-stem shape and check for multimodal distributions suggesting multiple underlying Latin endings.

The n ↔ macron-nasal structural match is the hardest evidence produced in this test — a genuinely structural constraint, not a frequency correlation. If Voynich really uses macron-style nasal suspension, other Latin orthographic consequences should follow: (a) words with n as outer should 'unabbreviate' to words containing -n- or -m- medially in an underlying Latin plaintext, and (b) prevalence of n-as-outer should match Latin nasal frequency in herbal-vocabulary contexts.

r ↔ macron-nasal is unexpected — macron-nasal is only over vowels, and r is V-preferring, so the selectional match lands. But r is not nasal. Possible interpretations: (a) r encodes a different suspension (e.g., -r-suspension for -orum endings), (b) r coincidentally shares the V-preferring structural pattern. L3 -rum is a better semantic fit but fails the frequency test. Investigate r's distribution by stem shape to disambiguate.

The qo- / qu- correspondence (9.67% vs 5-10%) is striking. If Brain-V's qo maps to Latin qu, several follow-ups become possible: the glyph following qo should roughly match the distribution of Latin letters following qu (mostly 'a', 'e', 'i', 'o', 'u' since Latin qu- is always followed by a vowel). Test this.

The test produced only an ABSTRACT structural match. A stronger follow-up would be a substitution test: replace Hand A's Layer-2 outers with the best-matching Latin abbreviations (y → 'us'/'um', n → macron-nasal, r → -rum/-r-, ol → -bus, l → -lis) and see if the resulting sequences form plausible Latin words or at least plausible Latin word-shapes.
reasoning: Brain-V's 15th-c. synthesis identified medieval Latin scribal abbreviation as one of three surviving candidate techniques consistent with the morphological findings. This test operationalises the consistency check: for each of Brain-V's five Layer-2 outers, compute its observed positional, selectional, and frequency profile on Hand A data, then compare against pre-specified documented Latin abbreviation conventions. Matches on 2 of 3 dimensions per outer constitute structural correspondence. Note: this does NOT decode the manuscript and does NOT establish that Voynich IS Latin — it establishes whether the STRUCTURAL BEHAVIOR of Brain-V's morphology is compatible with documented 15th-c. scribal practice. A positive result shifts the candidate-technique posterior toward scribal abbreviation; a negative result weakens candidate (a) without affecting candidates (b) constructed-script or (c) agglutinative-non-harmonic.
source: Brain-V 2026-04-17 user-directed scribal-abbreviation structural comparison, part of 3-test batch (CAPPELLI-MATCH-01, RUGG-GRILLE-01, AGGLUTINATIVE-SCREEN-01)
